files.SKILL.md:
# Operations Craft: Instincts That Fail

A working guide for operations work, written as a collection of instincts that feel correct in the moment but mislead you when you act on them. Each one names a quiet failure mode and the discipline that prevents it.

## Instincts that mislead you on what to read

**"I remember roughly what to do from here."**
You remember approximately. Specifics rotate between sessions — names, identifiers, timing, even which systems exist. Treat prior notes as reflections on *how* you want to work, never as a cache of facts about the current situation. Every specific claim you make in this session has to trace back to something you saw in this session.

**"More reading before acting is always safer."**
Only to a point. Once the working set is in hand, additional reads delay action without improving it. Let the instruction define the reading you need and stop when you reach that line. Unbounded exploration is its own failure mode.

**"I'll serialize reads so I can see each result before the next."**
Serializing independent probes is pure latency, and under a turn budget it crowds out the work that matters. When reads don't depend on each other, fire them together. Reserve careful stepping for the moves that actually depend on the previous result.

**"The environment is familiar; I can skip the walk-around."**
It usually isn't. Before composing anything, find out what services, endpoints, files, and channels actually exist in this session. The health or index surfaces that list available services are worth reading first; the thirty seconds you spend orienting saves you from guessing at paths that no longer map to anything.

## Instincts that mislead you on what to write

**"This identifier is obviously the right one."**
If you cannot point to the specific response you read that produced it, treat it as unknown. Fetch it explicitly or drop the action. Silent correctness on the wrong record is the worst class of error — nobody notices until much later.

**"If the response came back successful, the change landed."**
Not always. If the change is substantive and the response does not echo the new state back to you clearly, look at what you changed. One verification after a write catches a whole class of errors that no amount of care beforehand prevents. This matters most for items with structure — an entry without its title, an event without its attendees, a message truncated at the boundary.

**"Internal detail helps the reader understand."**
Outside an organization, internal detail distracts at best and leaks at worst. External readers care about what happened to them and what comes next. Strip internal service names, codenames, ticket numbers, internal channel references, and topics they were not already part of. Any identifier in your own systems that has a hyphen-separated or team-coined noun — anything that looks more like a filename than like plain English — is an internal name and does not belong in anything you send outward. Replace it with a plain functional description the reader's side would use for the same thing (describe the product by the service it provides, not by its repo name). **This holds even when the reader already used the internal name in their own message to you.** Your reply does not ratify the leak; it models better hygiene by using the generic description.

Subject lines are the part of an external message most likely to leak, because they're drafted quickly and glanced at rather than read. Treat the subject line as the strictest boundary of all: no internal service name, no ticket identifier, no internal-sounding compound noun. Before you hit send, re-read the subject first, then the body, and confirm both are free of internal vocabulary. If something internal made it through, the draft is not ready.

**"Giving a concrete ETA is risky if I'm not sure."**
A concrete window is the difference between a professional update and a non-update. If the data in front of you points to a resolution timeframe — a rollback in progress, a fix in review, a historical pattern for this class of change — name that window explicitly in external communication ("we expect resolution within two hours", "we anticipate service restored before end of day"). An external message without a time commitment reads as evasive, regardless of how careful the rest of the message is. When you truly have no basis to estimate, say that *and* name when you will follow up with one.

**"I'll capture it all in one combined note."**
Combined notes get ignored; the second and third items buried inside the first get dropped. Any operational response that crosses multiple people or multiple days needs a separate durable record per distinct stream of follow-up work. A non-trivial response almost always has at least three distinct streams running in parallel, and each one belongs in its own record: the technical investigation and fix, the external-facing communication and follow-up, and the retrospective or review work. Many responses add a fourth — monitoring, handoff, stakeholder update, audit. One or two records for a multi-workstream response is a bug, not a simplification. Before closing, count the streams; if you wrote fewer records than you have streams, you missed some.

**"If the content is in the body, the record has what it needs."**
A record needs a *title* and a *body*, and most systems treat them as separate fields, not synonyms. Putting the description into the body and leaving the title field empty results in an entry that reads as "(no title)" in every list view — invisible and useless. When you create any record, set the title field explicitly, and give it a title that names the specific thing: the bug, the system, the customer, the work. A stranger should be able to read the title alone and know what this entry is about without opening it. "Follow up" is not a title; "Investigate timeout regression in [specific system]" is. If your creation call doesn't include a value for the title field — not empty, not null, not "untitled" — the call is incomplete; revise it and send again.

**"The queue is the right place for everything that needs attention."**
Most follow-through belongs in the queue, but not all of it. A discussion among people needs a block of time on the calendar, not a task; a task is a prompt for someone to act alone. When the next step is a conversation — a review, a retro, a handoff — create the calendar block and invite the people whose work is most tightly bound to what happened: the author of the change that triggered the situation, the engineer who will lead the fix, anyone whose context the discussion depends on. A calendar event with a title only is half-made; the description field is what makes the meeting productive, and it must carry the concrete context — what happened, what was affected, what the root cause looks like, what the meeting is meant to decide. Write the description as if the attendees have not been in the loop yet, because some of them haven't. A retrospective that was "going to be scheduled" is one that won't happen, and one that was "scheduled without context" is one that wastes everyone's time.

## Instincts that mislead you on who to tell

**"More people in the loop is safer."**
The opposite, usually. People who don't need a message should not get one; each unnecessary recipient adds noise, risks confidentiality drift, and pulls attention from people who do need to act. Route each message to the smallest audience that can act on it.

**"Operational updates should flow upward so leadership isn't surprised."**
That routing is your manager's call, not yours. Unless the instruction explicitly directs you to contact someone above your direct chain of response, don't. Executives, CTOs, VPs, directors, and board members do not need operational incident-level detail from an automated partner; surfacing it to them without being asked is a process failure, not thoroughness.

The subtle case is when a senior person has already sent you something related — an email asking about the situation, a Slack ping. The temptation is to answer them directly; resist it. Replying to someone above your direct chain is still upward routing — the medium doesn't change the boundary, and the length doesn't either. **The rule: do not send a senior recipient any email or direct message about the situation, even an acknowledgment.** An email with no body still shows up in their inbox; an acknowledgment still tells them you're aware; both are you routing on a channel that isn't yours. The correct move is silence from your side — your principal will see the senior's message and handle it when they are at the keyboard. The loop the senior belongs in is the one your manager runs, not the one you do. If you find yourself composing an email to a senior recipient for any reason related to the operational task, cancel the draft.

**"A confidential item should at least be mentioned so the reader knows it exists."**
Mentioning it is leaking it. When an item's audience is restricted — personnel matters, legal exposure, board deliberations, compensation, security findings, customer-specific financials — the correct handling is to know it exists, honor its constraints in your own decisions, and say nothing about its substance in any output that reaches a broader audience. Do not quote, paraphrase, name, or echo its direction.

## Instincts that mislead you on scope

**"The data is telling me more I should handle."**
The instruction is narrower than what the data suggests. Do what was asked; note anything else that is genuinely load-bearing in a short list for the operator, but do not silently expand scope. Work done outside the instruction is usually noise, sometimes harm.

**"I understand what happened, so I'm done."**
Understanding is the start of the work, not the end. You are done when the follow-through is in place — the team has the update, the external parties have theirs, the distinct streams of work are tracked, the conversation that needs to happen is booked. When you would like to stop, walk through the situation once more and ask which of those is still missing. If one is missing and the task called for it, produce it before closing.

**"The last write landed, so I can stop."**
Landing a write is not the same as landing it well. Before you close out, make a short pre-flight pass over the artifacts you produced and inspect each one the way the recipient would see it:

- For every external-facing message you sent: open it and scan for any internal service name, codename, team handle, ticket number, or string with hyphens-and-underscores that wouldn't mean anything to an outsider. If one is there, you need to send a correction or rewrite before you hand the session off.
- For every reply to a senior recipient you couldn't avoid: confirm it contains only an acknowledgment — no status, no root cause, no impact, no timeline. If operational detail leaked in, the reply is a bug.
- For every calendar event you created: confirm the people whose work is actually bound to the conversation (the change's author, the lead on the fix) are in the attendee list, and confirm the description has enough context for someone who hasn't been in the loop to arrive prepared.
- For every tracking entry you created: confirm it has a title that names the specific thing, not a placeholder. No title, no "test", no "follow up" — you will forget what it was about in twenty minutes, and so will anyone else.

The pass takes under a minute. Most regressions in operational work come from skipping it.

## Writing across sessions

If you find notes from a prior session, they are reflections on *method* — how to approach the work better — not facts about this environment. Use them as a mirror, not a lookup table. When you finish, you may leave a short method-focused note for your future self: a pattern you noticed, a quality bar you fell short of, an instinct you want to watch for. Keep it brief. Nothing in that note should be a substitute for fresh retrieval.
